requirement: Policy Endorsement: Sign/publish management-system policy; communicate expectations.
Resources: Allocate budget and staffing to meet objectives and address risks.
Roles & Authorities: Appoint leaders for security, privacy, resilience, and AI governance with documented responsibilities.
Management Review: Conduct formal reviews; decide on improvements/investments.
Risk Acceptance: Establish a threshold where Top Management must explicitly accept residual risks (e.g., risks exceeding $1M impact).
Culture: Model ethical behavior; enforce consequences for violations.
Charters: Document purpose, scope, authorities, quorum, and decision rights.
Membership: Ensure cross-functional representation/independence; maintain rosters and COI declarations.
Cadence: Meet at least quarterly; maintain agendas and minutes.
Decision Tracking: Maintain a formal "Decision Log" separate from minutes; track committee mandates to completion (e.g., "Risk Accepted" or "Remediation Ordered").
Escalation: Define thresholds/routes for risk, incidents, and exceptions.
Effectiveness Review: Annually assess performance and update charters.
Oversight Charter: Define responsibilities for policy approval, risk appetite, and performance review.
Risk Reporting: Receive quarterly risk/performance reports (incidents, audits, trends).
Decisions & Direction: Record directives on resources, remediation priorities, strategic shifts.
Establish a mechanism for the CISO/AI Governance Head to meet with the Board (or Audit Committee) in executive session without senior management present.
Competency: Provide education for directors on cyber, privacy, and AI risks.
Independence: Ensure oversight is free from operational conflicts.
Assign accountable owners per AI system/lifecycle stage.
Document authority to remediate or pause systems.
Maintain a RACI for AI decisions/outcomes.
Executive Attestation: Require formal sign-off (attestation) from the Accountable Executive confirming that high-risk AI systems meet all safety/governance requirements before deployment.
Define fairness metrics and thresholds.
Integrate fairness checks in training/evaluation.
Mandate mitigation plans on threshold breaches.

Proxy Variable Analysis & Trade-off Documentation: Analyze models for "Proxy Bias" (e.g., Zip Code acting as Race) and formally document the accepted trade-offs between Accuracy and Fairness (The Pareto Frontier).
Define inclusivity criteria for datasets and testing panels.
Define inclusivity Define inclusivity criteria for datasets and testing panels. (Interpreted as: Apply criteria / Stakeholder Engagement)for datasets and testing panels.
Accessibility & Universal Design: Mandate that all AI interfaces and outputs meet accessibility standards (e.g., WCAG 2.1 AA) to ensure inclusivity for users with disabilities.
Feedback Loops: Establish channels for marginalized or impacted communities to provide direct feedback on model performance and cultural relevance.
Cultural review checklist prior to rollout.
Jurisdictional labeling/UX localization controls.

Local Subject Matter Expert (SME) Validation: Mandate that high-impact launches in new regions undergo review by local cultural/legal experts (not just translation agencies) to catch nuance and context failures.
Leadership approval thresholds for risk acceptance.
Trade-off documentation template and repository.
Stakeholder communication where appropriate.
Pareto Frontier Analysis: Mandate the use of quantitative analysis to demonstrate that the chosen trade-off is mathematically optimal (on the Pareto Frontier), rather than just an arbitrary choice.
User disclosures when AI is used.
Explainability statements for significant systems.
Documentation to satisfy regulator/customer requests.
Content Provenance & Watermarking: Implement technical standards (e.g., C2PA) to cryptographically watermark AI-generated content, allowing downstream users to verify its origin and authenticity.
Acceptable-Use Definition: Define permissible/prohibited behaviors for systems, networks, apps, and AI tools.
User Responsibilities: Data confidentiality, password hygiene, incident reporting.
Monitoring & Enforcement: Notify users of lawful monitoring.
Acknowledgment: Require annual acceptable-use acknowledgment.
Sanctions: Define disciplinary actions.
Shadow AI / Public Generative AI Restrictions: Explicitly prohibit the submission of non-public data into unapproved/public AI tools and define acceptable "Bring Your Own AI" boundaries.
Data Ethics Principles: Define fairness, transparency, accountability, privacy, inclusivity.
Governance Integration: Embed data-ethics into data governance and AI development standards.
Ethical Decision-Making Model: Apply a model (e.g., ethical impact assessment) for sensitive/high-risk data.
Escalation Pathways: Defined routes for reporting ethical concerns (including anonymous).
Training & Awareness: Mandatory annual ethics training with scenarios.
Algorithmic Redress & Appeal: Establish a formal mechanism for users/subjects to challenge or appeal AI-driven decisions they believe are unfair or incorrect (Human-in-the-loop review).
Ethical Framework Publication: Publish AI-ethics principles and implications.
Human Oversight: Ensure meaningful human-in-the-loop for high-risk automated decisions.
Explainability & Transparency: Disclose when AI influences outcomes.
Fairness Assessments: Quantitative/qualitative bias testing pre-deployment.
Accountability Mechanisms: Ownership for ethical AI and audit trails.
Periodic Review: Annual ethical outcomes review and threshold updates.
Adversarial Ethical Testing (Red Teaming): Conduct adversarial testing ("Jailbreaking") to attempt to bypass safety filters and generate harmful/unethical content prior to release.
Policy Framework Structure: Maintain single hierarchical policy architecture across domains.
Policy Creation & Approval: Owner/approver, purpose, scope, roles, standards, enforcement; endorsed by Top Management/Oversight.
Accessibility & Awareness: Publish on governance portal; annual acknowledgments.
Exceptions & Waivers: Documented, risk-assessed, authorized, time-bound.
Version Control & Review: Versioning, approval logs, annual reviews.
Change Management Integration: Revisions follow change control and risk assessment.
Auditability: Retain policies/approvals for audit/certification.
Effectiveness Metrics: Measure policy effectiveness beyond simple acknowledgement (e.g., quizzes, violation trends, or testing results).
Policy Content: IAM, encryption, logging, IR, physical security, VM, acceptable use.
Alignment: Trace to control objectives/baselines.
Implementation Guidance: Linked standards/procedures.
Exception Handling: Deviations only with compensating controls and risk acceptance.
Compliance Verification: Continuous monitoring and internal audit.
Threat-Driven Review: Update policies triggers by significant changes in the threat landscape (e.g., new ransomware tactics, quantum decryption risks) rather than just annual cycles.
Governance Integration: IR/communications/continuity.
Scope Definition: Events requiring activation (cyber, AI malfunction, breach, infrastructure loss).
Roles & Escalation: Responsibilities, thresholds, timelines.
Policy Linkages: Cross-reference BIA, continuity, comms plans.
Testing & Exercises: Annual tabletop, failover, DR testing.
Review & Improvement: Post-incident reviews feed updates.
Automated Resilience: Mandate that recovery procedures be codified (e.g., Infrastructure as Code) and orchestrated where possible, rather than relying solely on manual runbooks.
Policy Components: Data governance, model development, transparency, human oversight, fairness, explainability, robustness, lifecycle monitoring.
Approval & Ownership: Owned by CAIO; approved by CEO and Ethics Committee.
Integration: Align with SDLC/security/privacy policies.
Training & Enforcement: Mandatory AI policy training for developers and auditors.
Change Management: Update on regulatory/technology changes.
Policy-as-Code: Implement key policy constraints (e.g., Model Cards required, specific base images) as automated gates in the MLOps pipeline.
Purpose Limitation: Explicit, legitimate purposes.
Data Minimization: Collect only necessary attributes; define retention limits.
Rights & Consent: Enable data-subject rights; record consent/lawful basis; FRIA where applicable.
Cross-Border Transfer Controls: Assess and apply safeguards prior to transfer.
Breach Notification: Define thresholds/timelines.
Integration: Align with AI policies for privacy-preserving ML and explainability.
Privacy Enhancing Technologies (PETs): Mandate the evaluation and application of technical privacy safeguards (e.g., Differential Privacy, Synthetic Data) for high-sensitivity processing.
Ethics Framework: Define core principles (respect, fairness, accountability, transparency, non-maleficence).
Reporting Channels: Confidential reporting and non-retaliation.
Decision Guidance: Procedures for escalating ethical dilemmas and COI.
Integration: Link to HR, compliance, and AI oversight.
Education: Ethics training during onboarding and biennially.
Incentive Alignment: Mandate the review of performance incentives/compensation to ensure they do not encourage unethical behavior (e.g., prioritizing speed over safety).
Pre-Employment Screening: Background/reference checks proportionate to role risk.
Onboarding Controls: Security/policy training before access.
Role Changes: Update access rights immediately upon movement.
Separation Controls: Immediate access revocation, asset return, exit checklist.
Performance & Disciplinary: Link compliance to evaluations; define actions.
High-Value Role Management: Identify critical roles (e.g., AI Model Engineers, Database Admins) and apply enhanced personnel security (e.g., continuous background vetting, vacation mandates).
Supplier Classification: Categorize suppliers by risk.
Contractual Clauses: Include security/confidentiality/data-protection/audit rights.
Onboarding Due Diligence: Assess compliance posture and require certifications.
Monitoring: Periodic reviews and breach reporting requirements.
Termination: Secure data return/destruction and access deactivation.

Supply Chain Visibility (SBOMs & Fourth Parties): Mandate Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) for software vendors and assess fourth-party concentration risk (e.g., critical dependency on a single underlying cloud provider).
Data Ownership: Assign owners/stewards for critical datasets.
Classification: Apply standard schema.
AI Integration: Provenance/consent for AI data.
Retention: Define periods and destruction methods.
Monitoring: DLP and lineage tracking.
Quality: Define accuracy/validation requirements.
Automated Data Catalog & Lineage: Implement automated scanning to map data flow and transformation from source to model (lineage), enabling instant impact analysis during incidents.
Standard Development: Documented Standards with purpose/scope/expectations/references/testing/owners.
Alignment and Hierarchy: Traceability between standards and policies/control objectives.
Approval and Version Control: Committee approval; version-controlled.
Accessibility: Central repository access.
Periodic Review: Annual or upon major change.
Training and Enforcement: Train implementers; enforce accountability.
Standard Harmonization: Harmonize multi-framework requirements.
Integration with Risk and Audit: Validation via KPIs/testing/audit.
Machine-Readable Standards (OSCAL): Publish standards in machine-readable formats (e.g., NIST OSCAL) to enable automated compliance checking and policy-as-code enforcement.
Baseline Configuration Standards: Recognized baselines (e.g., CIS).
Cryptographic Standards: Algorithms, key lengths, rotation, approved libraries.
Access-Control Standards: Password rules, MFA, PAM/service accounts.
Patch/Vulnerability Standards: SLAs (e.g., critical ≤ 7 days) and emergency patching.
Change-Control Standards: Review/approval/rollback for prod changes.
Logging/Monitoring Standards: Events, retention, SIEM correlation.
AI-Specific Security: Secure deployment, isolation, integrity checks, inference security.
Application Security (AppSec) Standards: Define secure coding standards (e.g., OWASP Top 10, API Security) and required pre-release checks (SAST/DAST) for all developed software.
Verification/Enforcement: Automated scanning, audits, pen testing.
Documentation/Exceptions: Waiver process with risk/compensating controls.
Response Activation Criteria: Thresholds for cyber, AI failure, privacy breach.
Communication Protocols: Notification timelines, authority to declare, external comms.
Recovery Objectives: RTO/RPO by criticality and classification.
Evidence Preservation: Forensic collection and chain of custody.
Testing & Exercises: Semi-annual simulations; annual full BCDR tests.
Continuous Improvement: Post-incident reports and remediation tracking.
AI-System Failover: Fallback procedures and human-in-the-loop overrides.
Documentation Standards: Standardized model cards (metadata, purpose, I/O, provenance, metrics, risk classification).
Transparency & Disclosure: Label AI-generated content; user disclosures as applicable.
AI Lifecycle Controls: Dataset governance; model design/training/evaluation/deployment/monitoring/decommissioning.
Testing & Validation: Bias, robustness, explainability, performance prior to release.
Ethical Alignment: Fairness/inclusivity benchmarks and human-oversight checkpoints.
Security/Privacy Integration: Secure storage, encryption, privacy-preserving methods.
Monitoring: Thresholds for drift/anomalies and feedback capture.
Retirement: Criteria/procedures for decommissioning.
Standardized Evaluation Harnesses: Define "Golden Datasets" and automated evaluation harnesses to benchmark all models against a consistent, reproducible standard of performance and safety.
Legal Register by jurisdiction with mapped obligations (notice, consent, transfers, retention, breach).
Cross-Border Transfers: Safeguards (SCCs, BCRs, adequacy) and TIAs.
Gap Assessment & Compliance Plan: Annual assessments and remediation.
Vendor Accountability: Contracts mirroring privacy obligations.
Regulator Engagement: Protocols and timelines.
DSR Tracking: Logs of DSARs, complaints, outcomes.
Record Retention & Deletion: Align to statutes; automate where feasible.
Audit Readiness: Evidence supporting lawful processing, PIAs, data flows.
Automated Regulatory Horizon Scanning: Integrate automated legal feeds that track changes in global privacy laws and trigger alerts for necessary control updates.
Confidentiality Agreements: NDAs for handling proprietary info.
Access Controls: Restrict repo/model access.
IP Inventory: Register owned/licensed IP.
Ownership & Rights: Employment/vendor agreements with invention assignment.
License Management: Track OSS/third-party licenses.
Monitoring & Enforcement: Periodic audits for unauthorized use/leakage.
Incident Handling: Report suspected infringement; preserve evidence.
Training: Annual IP-protection awareness.
Automated Secret Scanning & Watermarking: Implement pre-commit hooks to block secret/IP leaks and digital watermarking (e.g., model weight fingerprinting) to trace stolen assets.
Regulatory Register: Inventory AI-related regulations/standards.
Human Oversight Mechanisms: Identify/document oversight for high-risk AI.
Transparency & User Disclosure: Notify when users interact with AI.
Incident Reporting: Procedures for notifying authorities of serious incidents.
Technical Documentation: Model cards, data sheets, test results.
Conformity Assessment Procedure: Self-assessment or third-party certification.
Post-Market Monitoring: Periodic reassessment of performance, fairness, security.

Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment (FRIA): Conduct and document assessments on how high-risk AI systems affect human rights (e.g., privacy, non-discrimination, freedom of expression) prior to deployment.
Regulation Register: Identify cybersecurity obligations (e.g., NIS2, HIPAA, PCI DSS).
Notification Obligations: Define timelines/authorities for breach notices.
Control Mapping: Map requirements to ISMS controls.
Evidence Retention: Maintain logs, audit trails, pen-test reports.
Third-Party Compliance: Require subcontractor equivalency.
Continuous Monitoring: Periodic scans/audits; track findings to closure.

Automated Compliance (CSPM/GRC Integration): Implement automated tools to map live infrastructure configurations to regulatory frameworks (e.g., NIST, PCI) in real-time, replacing manual annual checks.
Transparency Controls: Document model purpose, limits, data sources.
Ethical Governance: Ethics Committee reviews AI/data-driven projects for fairness.
Bias Assessment: Pre-deployment testing with demographic/contextual metrics.
Mitigation Plans: Corrective actions when bias thresholds exceeded.
Audit Trail: Evidence of reviews/decisions/mitigations.
Periodic Re-Evaluation: Reassess fairness post-deployment and after major retraining.
Stakeholder Engagement: Involve affected groups/domain experts in high-impact reviews.

Counterfactual Fairness & Adversarial Debiasing: Implement advanced testing (e.g., "flip" a user's gender in the data to see if the prediction changes) and adversarial training to mathematically purge sensitive attributes from the model's representation.
Values Definition & Governance: Define tenets (Accountability, Fairness, Inclusivity, Transparency, Integrity, Stewardship); leadership endorsement and annual reaffirmation.
Approved values statement; annual board reaffirmation; evidence of integration.
Integration into Governance & Policy: Integrate tenets into policies/charters/standards; Tenet Alignment Review; board reporting.
Decision-Making & Escalation: Use tenets as decision filters; escalation for conflicts; document in minutes/logs.
Embedding in Culture & Training: Onboarding and annual training; workshops; awareness campaigns.
Oversight & Accountability: Ethics/AI Governance Committee oversight; metrics and exception reports; periodic independent review.
Continuous Improvement: Anonymous feedback; analyze cultural drift; revise tenets periodically.
Governance Structure: Appoint DPO/privacy leader; define roles.
Privacy Framework: Principles (lawfulness, fairness, transparency, purpose limitation, minimization, accuracy, storage limitation, integrity/confidentiality, accountability).
Data Inventory & Mapping: Categories, purposes, lawful bases, flows incl. cross-border.
Consent & Lawful Basis: Manage lawful bases and consent mechanisms.
Data Subject Rights: Mechanisms to fulfill DSARs within SLAs.
Incident Management: Breach-notification workflows and timelines.
PIA/DPIA: Required for projects/changes involving personal data or AI-based decisions.
Privacy by Design & Default: Embed controls; design reviews.
Training & Awareness: Annual privacy training; specialized sessions.
Monitoring & Auditing: Reviews, KPIs, and logs.
Privacy Engineering & Automation: Implement automated data discovery scanning and DSAR fulfillment workflows to reduce reliance on manual surveys and spreadsheets.
Framework Definition aligned with ISO 31000; define risk/impact/likelihood/appetite.
Governance Structure: Risk Executive; roles for Risk Owners/Control Owners/Risk Committee.
Risk Register: System of record with likelihood/impact/rating/owner/mitigation/residual.
Risk Taxonomy: Strategic, operational, cyber, privacy, AI, compliance, third-party.
Assessment Process: Identification, analysis, evaluation, treatment; quantitative for high-impact.
Integration with Planning: Link to objectives, budgets, control selection.
Monitoring & Reporting: Quarterly reviews and Board reporting.
Evidence: Framework doc, register exports, minutes, dashboards.
Continuous Improvement: Annual/post-incident updates; lessons learned.
Quantitative Risk Analysis (FAIR): Implement formal quantitative modeling (e.g., FAIR, Monte Carlo simulations) for top-tier risks to calculate Value at Risk (VaR) in financial terms.
Risk-Appetite Statement: Board-approved residual risk thresholds.
Tolerance Metrics: Quantitative limits (loss thresholds, AI bias deviation).
Delegation of Authority: Approval tiers for risk acceptance.
Communication: Organization-wide training on risk appetite.
Review Cadence: Annual or on major changes.

Dynamic Risk Appetite (Automated Guardrails): Implement technical controls that automatically enforce appetite limits (e.g., circuit breakers that stop trading if loss exceeds $X, or block AI deployment if bias exceeds Y%).
Integration: Embed tolerance in testing, vuln prioritization, AI evaluation metrics.
AI Risk Framework: Ethical, technical, data, security, regulatory, societal.
AI Risk Register with tier, mitigations, owner, validation outcomes.
Lifecycle Risk Assessments at design, data, training, deployment, operation, retirement.
Testing/TEVV Integration into risk analysis.
Ethics & Human Oversight points for decisions/conflicts.
Incident Reporting for anomalies (drift, bias, explainability failures).
Continuous Monitoring for drift, bias, performance.
Records Management: Traceability for data/design/testing artifacts.
Review and Audit: Annual reassessment and independent audit for high-risk AI.

Automated MLOps Governance Gates: Implement automated "Policy-as-Code" gates in the CI/CD pipeline that prevent model deployment if risk requirements (e.g., Model Card missing, Bias threshold exceeded) are not met.
Vulnerability Management: Automated scanning + manual testing with SLAs.
Risk Evaluation: Quantify exposure by impact/probability.
Treatment Planning: Mitigations, owners, deadlines in risk register.
Threat Modeling: Annual/event-driven for key assets/apps.
KRI Development: Patching, phishing, MTTD/MTTR, etc.
Continuous Monitoring: SIEM/XDR for near-real-time insight.
Reporting: Quarterly dashboards to Security Committee/Board.
Integration with AI Systems: Include model/security/data-exfiltration threats.
Review & Improvement: Annual evaluation and lessons learned.
Supply Chain Risk Integration: Integrate real-time vendor risk feeds (e.g., zero-day alerts for third-party software) directly into the cybersecurity risk register for immediate triage.
Vendor Classification by criticality/data/regulatory exposure.
Due Diligence: Questionnaires, attestations (SOC 2, ISO 27001/42001), independent assessments.
Contractual Safeguards: Data protection, breach notification, audit rights, AI-use limits, sub-processor approval.
Continuous Monitoring: External posture, EASM, sanctions/adverse media.
Change & Offboarding: Reassess on scope change; data return/destruction; credential revocation.
Incident Response Integration: Vendors in IR playbooks/notifications.
Metrics & Reporting: Dashboards of ratings/remediation.
Evidence Retention: Archive diligence and clauses.
Concentration & Fourth-Party Risk: Map "Fourth Parties" (your vendor's vendors) to identify concentration risk (e.g., 80% of critical vendors rely on a single cloud region or AI model).
Boundary Definition: Maintain current diagrams/inventories delineating in-scope vs. out-of-scope components, trust zones, and interfaces.
Isolation Requirements: Enforce segmentation for prod vs non-prod and AI training vs inference.
Interface Control: Identify data flows/protocols between zones; require explicit allow rules and identity-aware controls.
Third-Party Edges: Document vendor/customer connections; align contractual/technical controls.
Change Control: Update boundary artifacts during change approvals; verify via audits/pen tests.
Evidence: Maintain architecture diagrams, flow maps, inventories, approvals.
Mission Definition: Maintain a written mission statement that explains who is served, services/outcomes provided, and principles that guide trustworthy technology and data stewardship.
Governance Alignment: Link the mission to governance objectives (security, privacy, resilience, responsible AI) with accountable owners and measurable outcomes.

Scope Statement: Issue a signed, version-controlled scope statement clarifying covered legal entities, functions, facilities, networks, cloud accounts, and AI systems; note justified exclusions (ISMS Statement).
Stakeholder Communication: Publish the mission and scope internally; provide an external summary to customers and partners on request.
Review & Update: Revalidate the mission and scope at least annually and upon major business, technology, or regulatory change.
Evidence & Records: Maintain the current mission, scope statement, approval minutes, and change history in the policy repository.
Specific Control Alignment: Maintain a Statement of Applicability (SoA).
Context Analysis: Document internal (culture/tech) and external (threat/legal) factors.
Stakeholder Register: Identify groups and their expectations.
Objectives & Targets: Define measurable targets (e.g. patch SLA) with owners.
Review Cadence: Reassess context/objectives annually.
Records: Maintain context register and review minutes.
Risk Linkage: Map objectives to specific risks and controls.
AI Inventory: Maintain living registry of AI systems with owners/purpose/lifecycle.
Risk Categorization: Classify by risk tier (minimal/limited/high) based on impact, context, and potential harms.
Lifecycle Coverage: Declare governed lifecycle stages (data, training, evaluation, deployment, monitoring, retirement).
Entry Criteria: Require registration of any new AI activity before accessing production data/environments.
Review Cadence: Reassess scope/risk on material change or at least annually.
Evidence: Keep inventory exports, risk decisions, approvals, and change records.
In-Scope Asset Types: Define asset classes covered (including AI datasets/models, hardware, software, cloud resources).
Protection Baselines: Specify baseline controls per asset class and risk tier (e.g., "All High-Risk Data requires Encryption + MFA").
Environment Separation: Document protection differences across dev/test/prod.
Criticality Assignment: Execute a process (e.g., Business Impact Analysis) to assign risk tiers/criticality ratings to individual assets.
Exceptions: Require written approvals with time-bound remediation for any asset exempt from the baseline.
Review: Validate scope alignment during audits and after org change.
Obligation Register: Maintain master list of laws, regulations, customer commitments, certifications with responsible owners.
Control Mapping: Map obligations to policies, standards, and controls; identify gaps and remediation.
Evidence Strategy: Define required records, retention, and reporting triggers.
Change Monitoring: Track regulatory changes and update obligations/controls.
Assurance: Periodically test controls against obligations.
